What I looked at back on that day in March I said the improving earnings estimates and falling price for the rocket ship. How did I look at that? This is one of the charts we do at Earningscout. We measure the rates of change to the earnings and what we do is I don't care what the earnings estimates are I care if they're getting better or worse. So I never care if an earnings estimate for a company is $50 or minus 50. What we do is measure the percent change each week in those estimates and we measure those overall the quarterly and annual estimates to find out if the trends are getting better or worse. So what we show here is an earning score and this is end of March at the time cuz I want to show this is very important what was going on in late 2024 up until March when we did our presentation. Uh the earnings estimates in peak earning seasons for uh the second quarter of 2024, third quarter of 2024, and the fourth quarter of 24. In peak earning seasons, they were going down at increasing rates. And you might notice it looks like little they gets better, gets worse. What happens is about 80% of the S&P 500 reports within a 1 or 2 week period. And that's when the analysts change their estimates because that's when they get the guidance from the companies. So, what we saw in those three conse- two consecutive earning seasons was the estimates falling at increasing rates. And when that happens and the price keeps going higher, we flag that with an alligator jaws. Happened seven times over the last 35 years. And how we define that is two quarters in a row or more of weakening on a rate of change basis estimates for the S&P 500 and the price keeps going higher. And every time that's happened, it's always signaled a bad sign. And that's when we had that 19% drop in the S&P 500 in early 2025. And these are the last seven times, Ed, that the jaw has opened. The last time at the end of 2024 before the 19% sell-off when Trump escalated tariffs. But you can see it's predicted. So, in 2025, the estimates were still being cut, but they got cut at lesser rates. And this is what we saw in that March 30th presentation. The estimates were going up. Ed, we were going, "Hey, the estimates are going up at increasing rates, but the price was dropping." So, this was the reverse of an alligator jaw. Uh but they didn't listen to us. They went and lowered the Fed funds rate by 100 basis points and the bond yield went up 100 basis points. 2025, they lowered it by 75 basis points. So, now it doesn't make sense to us. Economy's doing fine. You haven't gotten the 2% yet. And the bond yield is the basically back at 4 and 1/2% So maybe worse gets it that if he uh you know he talks hawkish uh and maybe actually tightens by a quarter point uh that the bond market will love it and bond yield will come down and that's the the most important interest rate that when it comes to a lot of the activity in in the US. Nick, anything you want to add on that? >> I think you're right. It He being appointed by Trump, you just assume he's going to cut rates, right? That's how he gets the job. But he's doing the right thing and you mentioned Ed that the earnings growth is above 20% and I know you and I both believe this market can handle a hike or two uh to to cool things down if needed. I mean, the earnings trends are positive, the growth is high. Um I don't think the market people have to fear a rate hike is an automatic reason to sell as long as the earnings still stay strong and high and you can curb inflation. That's a that's a Goldilocks scenario. So I think that's >> one of the fears people have. They think Fed raising rates is an automatic thing to go bearish. It's no, that's a variable and how how does that impact earnings? And as long as they stay strong, you can stay bullish. >> Uh one last question from Michael. How much do the upward revisions uh in your work Nick have to slow for your system to provide a sell signal? >> That's a great question. It's all so we look at the revisions for the company on a rate of change how they're moving to itself and then relative to everything else in the database. So we're ranking. So we do a normal distribution in our indicator scores. We come up 1 to 100. So on those charts I was showing, we actually then have a paint by number indicator score for for clients and it's a percentile ranking in there. So we always have the same numbers of buys as we do sells. It's a normal distribution uh and typically what we're going to see is companies with the strongest estimate trends and weakening prices. Those are the ones we're going to want to buy as a rule of thumb and we're going to want to sell the ones where the estimate are consistently weakening and get and we think are going to continue to get worse on stocks that have been up 100, 2, 3, 400%. We're going to want to sell those and get out. So, it's a relative ranking system in there. Um so, how how we set it up. >> Nick, thanks very much. Uh it's always always fun doing this and getting an update uh right before the the earning season, kind of uh an insight into what the earning season might look like and this looks looks pretty promising, but uh first quarter was so kind of off the charts that we shouldn't expect uh something like that again, but uh we should be in good shape. Uh thanks again, everybody. Stay Stay cool. There's a big uh heat wave uh hitting the uh the US now uh after hitting Europe. Europe's supposed to get a break, I think, in in the next few days. So, I hope you're all enjoying the the the soccer and let's all enjoy uh July 4th and uh uh just uh just just have fun. Uh all the best, everybody. Thank you. Thanks, Nick. >> [music] [music]
